topic: difficult patient on placement who questions young physios

turns.content: had a rough one on placement today and i need to vent if thats alright
Go for it, that's what I'm here for. What happened?

so im treating this patient right. older guy maybe mid 50s. shoulder impingement nothing complicated. and before i even touch him hes like how old are you and i said 25 and he proper looked me up and down and said can i see someone more experienced

That's tough, especially when you haven't even had the chance to demonstrate what you know. It's unfortunately quite common on placement -- some patients just have a hard time trusting someone they perceive as young or junior. How did you handle it?

i kept it professional you know. told him i understand his concern and that my supervisor is overseeing everything and hes welcome to ask for someone else if he wants. but inside i was proper rattled init because ive been doing this for ages now i know what im doing with a shoulder impingement thats bread and butter

You handled that really well, honestly. Offering him the choice while staying calm and professional is exactly the right approach. It shows confidence without being defensive. Did he end up letting you treat him?

yeah he stayed but he was questioning every single thing i did. like why are you doing that what does that test show have you done this before. and i get it patients should ask questions thats fine but this wasnt curiosity it was like he was trying to catch me out

There's a real difference between a patient who's curious and engaged versus one who's testing you, and it sounds like you picked up on that. The thing is, every answer you gave him was quietly building credibility even if it didn't feel like it in the moment. When you explain the rationale behind a test clearly and confidently, most people start to relax. Did your supervisor see any of this?

she was in the room for part of it yeah. she said afterwards that i handled it well and the clinical reasoning was solid. which was nice to hear tbh. but its still annoying you know like i put in the same hours studying as everyone else. being young doesnt mean i dont know what im talking about

Absolutely, and that frustration is valid. Your supervisor backing you up is important -- hold onto that feedback because it's the objective assessment of your skill. The reality is this won't be the last patient who questions you based on your age, so it might help to develop a go-to line that establishes your credibility early. Something like briefly mentioning your training or the number of patients you've worked with before you start the assessment. Not defensive, just matter-of-fact.

yeah thats a shout actually. like setting the scene early so they know im not just some random. maybe something like ive been training for three years and ive seen a lot of shoulder presentations. keep it calm and factual. because arguing with them isnt going to help is it

Exactly. Something short and confident like that, dropped in naturally at the start, gives them a frame for how to see you. And you're right -- arguing never works. The patients who doubt you usually come around once they see you know your stuff. The ones who don't were never going to be convinced by words anyway. You just keep being competent and let the work speak.
icl though the worst part is when you go home and replay it in your head and think of all the things you should have said differently. like i know i did fine but my brain wont let it go you know

That post-session replay is incredibly common, especially for students and early-career clinicians. The fact that you're reflecting on it shows you care about doing well, which is a good quality. But try to balance it -- your supervisor said your clinical reasoning was solid, and the patient stayed and was treated. That's the outcome that matters. Give yourself the same credit you'd give a mate in the same situation.

yeah youre right. if one of my mates told me this id be like bruv you smashed it stop overthinking. i just need to take my own advice sometimes haha. alright im letting it go. thanks for listening to me rant about this

Anytime. For what it's worth, the fact that you stayed professional under pressure and got positive feedback from your supervisor says a lot. Each difficult patient is just practice for the next one. You'll be laughing about this by the time you qualify.
